value.market_read: 3-2-1 Crack: The 3-2-1 settled at $59.25/bbl, down $2.09 w/w, easing off the $61.61 print seen two weeks prior but holding well above the $45 range that dominated April–May. WTI settled at $67.84 and fell $2.50 on the week, so the crack compression came less from crude strength than from product settlements slipping — the June 26 report showed a 2.33 mb gasoline draw and a 2.48 mb distillate build, and the distillate restock capped product-side margin. With refinery utilization at a robust 96.6%, throughput is running flat-out into peak-season demand, which limits further crack upside from supply tightness. Watch: Whether the 96.6% run rate holds through July or Gulf Coast weather trims capacity, which would re-tighten the product balance. Gasoline Crack: The gasoline crack settled at $55.30/bbl, off $1.80 w/w. Despite a 2.33 mb gasoline stock draw for the week ending June 26 — a supportive summer-driving signal — the crack softened as RBOB settlements lagged the broader complex and crude held relatively firm on a $2.50 weekly decline. Gasoline exports at 1.02 mb/d are within normal range, so domestic barrels are neither being aggressively pulled offshore nor stranded at home. Watch: July 4 driving pull and the pace of gasoline draws — sustained draws against 96.6% runs would firm the crack back toward the recent $58–60 highs. Distillate Crack: The distillate crack settled at $67.13/bbl — the standout of the complex and the anchor of this distillate-led regime. The 2.48 mb distillate build for the week ending June 26 argues for some near-term give, but the crack has stayed elevated because starting inventories remain lean and diesel demand from freight and off-road summer activity is holding. This is the fattest crack in the barrel and is doing the heavy lifting on refinery margins. Watch: Whether distillate builds string together over coming weeks — a repeat 2+ mb build pattern would begin to erode this premium into late summer. Jet Crack: The jet crack settled at $50.27/bbl, the thinnest of the products but supported by peak summer air-travel demand. Refiners running at 96.6% face a regrade decision — with the distillate crack at $67.13 towering over jet at $50.27, the yield incentive tilts toward maximizing diesel/ULSD output over jet where units allow. That switching keeps jet supply adequate and caps the jet crack even into strong travel season. Watch: The diesel-jet regrade spread — as long as distillate cracks stay $15+ over jet, expect jet yields to stay pressured and the crack rangebound. Brent–WTI Spread: The spread settled at $3.10/bbl, a narrow print that reflects WTI holding its ground relative to Brent even as WTI fell $2.50 on the week. At this level the US export arb is weak — crude and product barrels are less competitive abroad, which tends to keep supply domestic and support US inventories. That said, crude exports at 4.01 mb/d remain healthy, so the narrow spread has not yet choked flows outright. Watch: For the arb to re-widen and reincentivize exports, WTI would need to fall relative to Brent — monitor whether continued weak product-side demand pressures WTI further.
value.chart_321: At $59.25, the 3-2-1 sits well above the 5-yr band and sharply higher year-on-year, extending a climb from the mid-$40s in April to low-$60s in mid-June before this week's $2.09 pullback. The trajectory is textbook seasonal — margins firmed into peak driving season with utilization pinned at 96.6% — but the move has been distillate-led rather than gasoline-led, an unusual signature for June. The pullback off $61.61 reflects product settlements softening faster than the $67.84 WTI crude anchor.
value.chart_gas: The gasoline crack at $55.30 is down $1.80 w/w but remains firm relative to spring lows, consistent with the July 4 driving pull and a supportive 2.33 mb gasoline draw for the week ending June 26. Summer-spec (low-RVP) gasoline economics and full 96.6% runs are keeping the crack elevated even as it gives back ground. Notably, gasoline is not leading margins this cycle — distillate is doing the work — which is atypical for early summer.
value.chart_dist: The distillate crack at $67.13 is the fattest in the barrel and the defining feature of this distillate-led regime, holding elevated despite a 2.48 mb build for the week ending June 26. Lean starting inventories plus resilient freight and off-road diesel demand are sustaining the premium. ULSD strength this far into summer — normally a shoulder season for diesel cracks that peak in winter — signals genuinely tight distillate balances heading toward heating season.
value.chart_jet: The jet crack at $50.27 is the thinnest product margin, held back by refiner yield economics rather than weak demand — summer air travel is at peak. With distillate cracks at $67.13 towering $17 over jet, units are incentivized to shift regrade toward diesel, keeping jet supply ample and the crack capped. Jet remains the swing yield that refiners flex against the more lucrative distillate pool.
value.chart_bw: The Brent–WTI spread at $3.10 is narrow, reflecting WTI holding relative to Brent even after a $2.50 weekly decline in WTI. A spread this tight weakens the US export arb — crude and refined products are less competitive into export markets — though crude exports at 4.01 mb/d show flows have not shut. For the arb to re-widen and restore export economics, WTI would need to soften further against Brent; the narrow spread itself points to already-firm WTI relative to the global benchmark rather than fresh upside.
